{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks which anticonvulsant causes contraction of the visual field, specifically referring to visual field loss. From the retrieved documents, multiple sources confirm that vigabatrin is associated with permanent peripheral visual field loss, particularly concentric visual field constriction (tunnel vision). Document [0] states that vigabatrin causes permanent peripheral visual field loss due to toxicity to retinal photoreceptors and ganglion cells. Document [2] explicitly mentions that vigabatrin can cause permanent bilateral concentric visual field constriction, which is a form of contraction of the visual field. Document [3] notes that peripheral visual field defects occur as early as 9 months and 11 months after initiating treatment and are commonly detected after 5 to 6 years. Document [5] also confirms that toxic optic neuropathy from vigabatrin results in bilateral concentric peripheral visual field loss. In contrast, levetiracetam (A) is noted for a favorable safety profile and no mention of visual field contraction. Phenytoin (B) is associated with CNS depression, nystagmus, and ataxia, but not specifically with visual field contraction. Ethosuximide (D) is used for absence seizures and has no reported association with visual field defects. Therefore, the only anticonvulsant among the options linked to contraction of the visual field is vigabatrin.", "answer_choice": "C"}
